hero.tagline:
Explore, preserve, and transmit
Algerian cultural heritage.

manifesto.title: The Manifesto
manifesto.quote:
Heritage does not disappear
when it is forgotten.
It disappears when it is no
longer transmitted.
manifesto.body: Traça was born from an urgent need: to make often remote, fragile, or invisible places accessible, and to anchor them in a living collective memory. An immersive platform dedicated to discovering historical and architectural sites through 3D experiences, documented narratives, and a digital museum approach.

mapTeaser.label: The Geographical Experience
mapTeaser.title: Algeria in Three Dimensions
mapTeaser.text: Travel through the 58 wilayas via an unprecedented interactive map. Filter by eras, themes, and discover the secrets of each historical site.

institutionalCta.label: An institutional vision
institutionalCta.title:
Traça aims to become
the digital museum of Algerian heritage.
institutionalCta.text: A bridge between places, knowledge, and generations — designed for cultural institutions, tourism players, and future generations.

hero.label: Our History
hero.title:
About
Traça
hero.text: Traça is an independent cultural project driven by a desire to preserve and transmit Algerian heritage. The platform aims to become a bridge between places, knowledge, and generations.

values.label: Our values
values.preservationText: Documenting, digitizing, and archiving heritage sites before they disappear — a cultural urgency and collective responsibility that Traça fully assumes.
values.accessibilityText: Making the remote, fragile, or invisible accessible — allowing everyone to walk through spaces that few can physically visit, without compromising the quality of the experience.
values.transmissionText: Anchoring the memory of places within a living collective memory, through rigorous, sensitive, and accessible storytelling for all generations, here and in the diaspora.

vision.label: Our Vision
vision.title:
We believe that technology can be
a tool for memory.
vision.text1: A means to document, transmit, and protect what is fragile. Traça is designed as a foundation for future collaborations with cultural institutions, schools, researchers, and tourism stakeholders.
vision.text2: The platform uses immersive 3D experiences directly in the browser, without an external application. The scenes are built using photogrammetric scans, architectural modeling, and in-depth historical research.
vision.quote: « Heritage does not belong to the past. It is alive, it breathes, it waits to be traversed. »

founder.label: The Founder
founder.name: Chahinez Fettaka
founder.role: Architect by training, writer, researcher, and passionate about art and culture.
founder.text1: Driven by a fierce desire to preserve architectural memory and the richness of forgotten narratives, Chahinez Fettaka founded Traça. Her ambition: to build a technological and poetic bridge between ancient History and future generations.
founder.text2: Her unique approach relies on a dual expertise: the rigor of the architectural approach and the sensitivity of documentary and narrative research. Through her work, she aims not only to freeze structures in time via immersive experiences but also to transcribe the soul and deep culture of Algerian heritage.

audience.label: Target Audiences
audience.institutionsText: Ministry of Culture, national museums, heritage directorates — institutional partners at the heart of the Traça project.
audience.tourismText: Tourist offices, specialized agencies, and digital platforms wishing to promote heritage beyond borders.
audience.academicText: Schools of architecture, faculties of history and art, researchers in archaeology and heritage digitization.
audience.publicText: The curious, travelers, Algerians of the diaspora — all those who wish to (re)discover their heritage.

faq.label: Frequently Asked Questions
faq.a1: Traça is an immersive cultural platform dedicated to the preservation and promotion of Algerian architectural heritage. Through 3D photogrammetry, we make historical sites accessible that are often remote, fragile or little-known, offering everyone the opportunity to pass through them virtually.
faq.a2: Very simple: 1) Start on the Explorer page to view all available sites. 2) Click on a place that interests you. 3) Read the presentation, explore the gallery and view the timeline. 4) Launch the 3D experience by clicking the activation button. No download or plugin required.
faq.a3: 3D experiences are integrated directly into the browser via WebGL (Sketchfab). From a location page, scroll down to the Immersive Experience section and click the launch button. A stable internet connection is recommended for optimal quality. No installation is necessary.
faq.q4: How to view articles and chronicles?
faq.a4: All chronicles and analyses are available on the Chronicles page. They are organized by theme and date. Each article is accompanied by historical context, academic references and images associated with the site.
faq.q5: How to become a partner or collaborate?
faq.a5: We welcome all forms of collaboration: cultural institutions, researchers, tourist offices, architecture schools. Contact us directly via the Contact page or at contact.Traça@gmail.com to discuss a partnership.

memory

hero.title:
Living
Memory
hero.sub: Recent excavations, 3D restorations, field studies, and major exhibitions — research on Algerian heritage, editorialized by Traça.

featured.label: Featured
featured.title:
Splendors Rediscovered:
The Khenchela Mosaic
featured.excerpt: 8 kilometers south of Babar, a team from the University of Constantine unearthed a 14 m² Roman mosaic panel depicting a Numidian hunting scene. The exceptional state of preservation suggests a patrician villa from the era of Septimius Severus. Traça participated in the photogrammetric documentation of the site.

secondary1.title: Mers Eddadjadj: Immersion into Medieval Daily Life
secondary1.excerpt: The 2024 excavations in Boumerdès reveal unprecedented stratigraphic layers of an 11th-century merchant city. Ceramics, Fatimides coins, and metal tools outline the daily life of an advanced urban civilization.

secondary2.title: The Art of Tassili: A Millennial Journey
secondary2.excerpt: The exhibition inaugurated at the Bardo Museum in 2025 presents for the first time high-resolution digital reproductions of 340 rock panels from Tassili n'Ajjer — a project co-financed with UNESCO and to which Traça contributed with its 3D models.

editorial.title:
Writing
History
now.
editorial.text: Every article published on Traça is the result of direct collaboration with Algerian archaeologists, historians, and cultural institutions. We don't just report history — we document it, at the source, as it is being written. Our texts are verified, sourced, and linked to the 3D experiences available on the platform.

card1.title: The Tomb of the Christian revisited
card1.excerpt: New thermoluminescence dating confirms a Numidian construction prior to the Roman period, questioning the attribution to Cleopatra Selene II.

card2.title: Ottoman fortresses of the Algerian coastline
card2.excerpt: A comparative study of fortification techniques between the 15th and 17th centuries reveals surprising local adaptations to the geological constraints of the Mediterranean coast.

card3.title: Digitizing before it's too late
card3.excerpt: According to an ICOMOS report, 34% of classified sites in Algeria are in critical danger. Traça documents the emergency digitization process on 3 threatened sites.

newsletter.label: Traça Newsletter
newsletter.title: Stay connected to History
newsletter.sub: An in-depth monthly article, new discoveries, and 3D experience releases — directly in your inbox.

body.p1: 8 kilometers south of Babar, a team of researchers from the University of Constantine unearthed what could be one of the most significant archaeological discoveries of the decade: a 14 m² Roman mosaic panel in an exceptional state of preservation.
body.p2: Depicting a Numidian hunting scene of great finesse, the artwork testifies to the cultural richness of the region during the era of Septimius Severus. The pigments, which remained vibrant despite centuries spent underground, depict hunters on horseback pursuing gazelles amidst complex geometric patterns.
body.p3: The Traça team had the privilege of participating in the photogrammetric documentation of the site. Using laser scanning and high-resolution photography technologies, we were able to create a digital twin of the mosaic, allowing it to be studied without risking damage through excessive physical manipulation.
body.p4: This discovery suggests the existence of a vast patrician villa still buried. Excavations are ongoing, and every day brings new clues about the daily life of the elites of the Numidia province at the height of the Roman Empire.

body.p1: The 2024 excavations at the Mers Eddadjadj site, near Boumerdès, revealed a little-known facet of Algerian medieval urbanism. This ancient and medieval port, mentioned by numerous Arab travelers, is finally bringing its secrets to light.
body.p2: Archaeologists have uncovered a dynamic port area with massive warehouses, indicating that Mers Eddadjadj was a crucial center of commerce in the 11th century, connecting the Algerian coasts to the rest of the Mediterranean and beyond.
body.p3: Among the most touching finds are everyday objects: carved bone combs, terracotta oil lamps with delicate patterns, and most notably, a large quantity of Fatimides ceramics whose metallic reflections testify to high-level craftsmanship.
body.p4: "It's not just a city we are uncovering, it's a way of life," explains one of the researchers on site. Traça collaborated with the team to digitize these objects, allowing visitors to our platform to manipulate them virtually and appreciate their finesse.

hero.title:
The Art of Tassili:
A Millennial Journey

body.p1: Tassili n'Ajjer, an arid plateau in southeastern Algeria, is home to one of the most important collections of prehistoric rock art in the world. Over 15,000 drawings and engravings are listed there, forming a true open-air library of human evolution.
body.p2: The exhibition inaugurated at the Bardo Museum in 2025 presents for the first time high-resolution digital reproductions of these masterpieces. This ambitious project, carried out in collaboration with UNESCO, aimed to document the most fragile panels, threatened by natural erosion.
body.p3: What is most striking in these frescoes is the presence of animals that have now disappeared from the region: giraffes, elephants, and even hippopotamuses. These images tell of an era when the Sahara was a fertile land, crisscrossed by rivers and inhabited by large populations of hunter-gatherers and later pastoralists.
body.p4: Traça contributed to this mission by creating 3D models of the most inaccessible sites. Thanks to laser scanning, we can today virtually fly over the canyons of Tassili and zoom in on details invisible to the naked eye, revealing the complex superimposition of typographic and artistic styles over the millennia.

hero.title:
Tomb of the
Christian
hero.subtitle: Kbor er Roumia — Royal Mausoleum of Mauretania

presentation.label: Presentation
presentation.title:
The enigma of stone
facing the Mediterranean
presentation.p1: Standing majestically at an altitude of 261 meters on the hills of Sidi Rached, in the Tipaza wilaya, the Tomb of the Christian dominates the Mitidja plain and the Mediterranean Sea. This monumental ashlar mausoleum is the pinnacle of Numido-Mauretanian funerary architecture.
presentation.p2: Most likely built under the reign of the scholar king Juba II and his wife Cleopatra Selene II, the edifice bears the popular Arabic name "Kbor er Roumia". This titanic circular monument symbolizes the union of a native Numidian heritage and an openness to Greek and Ptolemaic influences, forming a unique architectural work in the Maghreb.
presentation.p3: A silent but powerful witness to the greatness of the Kingdom of Mauretania, it has survived looting, naval bombardments, and the ravages of time. Recognized for its universal value, it has been on the UNESCO World Heritage list since 1982 and, more recently in April 2025, on the tentative list of "Royal Mausoleums".

histoire.label: Historical Overview
histoire.title:
The landmark of centuries
chronicle of an immortal monument
histoire.p1: The exact dating of the mausoleum remains uncertain. The most commonly accepted hypothesis, supported notably by the historian Stéphane Gsell, attributes it to the reign of Juba II (25 BC to 23 AD) or his son Ptolemy. The first written mention of the monument comes from the ancient geographer Pomponius Mela around the year 40, who poetically describes it as « monumentum commune regiae gentis » (the common monument of the royal family).

t1.detail: Early violation and disappearance of the funerary furnishings, shortly after its last use.

t2.detail: Construction commissioned by Juba II, King of Mauretania.

t3.detail: The geographer Pomponius Mela notes it in his writings (approx. 40 AD).

t4.detail: Attempted destruction to extract the treasure, repelled by a swarm of wasps.

t5.detail: New artillery breach attempt, failing against the robustness of the masonry.

t6.detail: The monument served as a training target for the cannons of the French navy.

t7.detail: Berbrugger and Mac Carthy discover the secret entrance and the interior architecture.

t8.detail: Official classification as a World Heritage Site by UNESCO.

t9.detail: Inscription on the global tentative list of "Royal Mausoleums".

legendes.label: Living Memory
legendes.title: On the borders of Myth
legendes.p: The hermetism of this impenetrable architecture has fed the popular imagination for centuries. Between inaccessible treasures and powerful spells, oral memory has shaped the aura of the place.

c1.title: The flying treasure
c1.text: An Algerian prisoner in Europe was freed by his magician master on the sole condition that he burn a talisman at the top of the tomb upon his return. Barely had the paper been consumed when the mountain of stone split open. A massive amount of gold flew towards Spain. In a desperate gesture to keep some of it, the former prisoner threw his burnous: the charm broke. The tomb closed heavily over the fault line, leaving him only the riches trapped under his golden garment, which were enough to make him a king among the rich of Algiers.
c1.quote: « Gold that defies time, gold that crosses the seas. »

c2.title: The cow and the shepherd
c2.text: At dusk, an intrigued herdsman noticed that his most beautiful cow was mysteriously escaping. He followed her discreetly into the night and discovered that she was entering the royal mausoleum through a fine opening hidden in the rock. Holding firmly onto her tail, he managed to slip inside. There, in the heart of darkness, piles of sparkling coins appeared before him. He grabbed as many as he could carry, emerging just in time before dawn, becoming the richest man of his lineage.
c2.quote: « The secret door that opens only to horned beasts. »

c3.title: Christian or Roman?
c3.text: The name "Tomb of the Christian" is said to be the result of a pure mistranslation. In Arabic, "Kbor er Roumia" means "The tomb of the Roman woman". The term Rûm encompassed Byzantines, Romans, and later Christians. The French opted for "Christian". Some also suggested that the name was due to the specific shape of the crossed moldings carved on the false doors, evoking a misleading Latin cross.
c3.quote: « An error etched in marble and minds. »

architecture.label: Engineering & Structure
architecture.title:
A titan of stone
sealed against the wind
architecture.extP1: Monumental and cyclopean, this stone tumulus contains nearly 80,000 cubic meters of materials. With a staggering diameter of 60.9 meters, it originally stood over 40 meters high (compared to the 32.4 meters preserved today). The edifice rests on a strict ternary composition.
architecture.extL1: The square base: measuring 63.4 meters on each side, a foundation anchoring the mausoleum into its natural platform.
architecture.extL2: The cylindrical body: with a circumference of 185.5 meters. It is clad in an architectural shell animated by 60 engaged columns whose capitals (now missing) supported an imposing cornice.
architecture.extL3: The stepped cone: the tiered roof initially formed of exactly 33 concentric circular courses narrowing towards the top.
architecture.extP2: At the four cardinal points stand false doors 6.9 meters high. The cross-shaped framing of these blind doors largely contributed to the myth of the "Christian" tomb. These dummy elements were intended to prevent violations, a heavy mineral optical illusion.
architecture.intP1: It was not until 1865, under Napoleon III, that Adrien Berbrugger probed the location of the true entrance: hidden on the side of the right base beneath the eastern false door. The access, barely 1.1 meters wide and closed by a sandstone slab found shattered, plunged into darkness.
architecture.acc1Text: At the upper entrance of the oblique corridor, one discovers the sculpted bas-reliefs of a lioness and a lion. A powerful monarchical symbol (associated with Juba II).
architecture.acc2Text: A long corridor about 2.5 m high carefully circling the internal monument, in the reassuring shadow of the thick masonry.
architecture.acc3Text: A space of 4m by 3m that was supposed to house the royal remains. Unfortunately found empty by Berbrugger following early Roman looting.

genese.label: Influences
genese.title:
The Ancestor of Numidia
the blending of an Empire
genese.p1: The Tomb of the Christian does not come out of nowhere. It is the culmination of a long archaic tradition of protohistoric burial under a tumulus (or bazina). But its great strength was to hybridize this indigenous Berber structure with the innovations of a rapidly expanding Hellenistic world.
genese.p2: There is a strong echo of Hellenistic features, similar to the famous Mausoleum of Augustus erected in Rome. However, historians claim that it is precisely the integration of pseudo-Greek columns (Ionic order) of Hellenistic inspiration onto a powerful pre-Roman circular tumular architecture that gives it its masterful appearance.
genese.p3: The historian Stéphane Gsell described the building as « a construction of an indigenous type, covered with a Greek shell ». A blending in which one also suspects the distant presence of Cleopatra Selene II, through certain symbols (notably the sculpted felines), mixing a spirituality sometimes from the banks of the Nile with purely African carved rock.
genese.encartText: The style and layout of the tomb are intimately linked to the Medracen of Batna. Erected on the lands of Aurès much earlier by the first great Numidian dynasty of Syphax and Massinissa, the Medracen is the architectural father and the inspirer of a long tradition of large-scale Algerian royal bazinas.

audioSec.label: Sonic Heritage
audioSec.title: Guided Narration — Kbor er Roumia
audioSec.quote: « Kbor er Roumia — The tomb of the Roman woman. No lost treasure, no forgotten Christian. Just a queen, a king, and the stone that withstands time. »
